threats.title: RCE via vm.runInContext sandbox escape in B2B order — routes/b2bOrder.ts
threats.description: The B2B order endpoint evaluates user-supplied `orderLinesData` using `notevil` sandboxed eval inside `vm.runInContext`. The notevil library is not a true security sandbox — it can be escaped via prototype pollution or constructor access. An attacker can achieve full server-side code execution by sending a crafted `orderLinesData` payload.
threats.controls_in_place: notevil sandboxed eval, 2000ms timeout — both bypassable

mitigation.title: Replace eval-based order processing with structured JSON parsing
steps: Remove vm.runInContext and notevil entirely from order processing
Define a strict JSON schema for orderLinesData and validate with ajv
Parse order lines as structured data, not executable code

threats.title: Denial of Service via B2B infinite loop — routes/b2bOrder.ts
threats.description: An attacker can craft an `orderLinesData` payload that causes an infinite loop inside notevil's evaluator. While notevil detects this and throws, the 2000ms execution timeout in vm.runInContext will block the Node.js event loop for 2 seconds per request, enabling a simple amplification DoS attack.

evidence.excerpt: if (utils.getErrorMessage(err).match(/Script execution timed out.*/) != null) { res.status(503) ... }

threats.controls_in_place: 2000ms timeout — event loop blocking during timeout

mitigation.title: Remove eval; use worker threads for isolated processing if needed
steps: Remove the eval-based processing entirely
If scripted order processing is required, use a worker_thread with a strict timeout and kill on timeout

threats.title: B2B API reachable by any authenticated user — server.ts
threats.description: The B2B order endpoint at /b2b/v2/orders is protected only by `security.isAuthorized()` — any authenticated user (including regular customers) can access it. The B2B API should only be accessible to specific B2B customer accounts with appropriate roles.

mitigation.title: Restrict B2B API to B2B customer role
steps: Create a B2B customer role in the User model
Update security.isAuthorized() to accept optional role parameter
Apply `security.isAuthorized('b2b')` to /b2b/v2 routes

threats.title: No audit trail for B2B orders — routes/b2bOrder.ts
threats.description: B2B orders processed through the /b2b/v2/orders endpoint are not logged with sufficient detail to reconstruct what orderLinesData was submitted. If an attacker abuses the RCE vulnerability, there is no forensic record of the malicious payload that was executed.

evidence.excerpt: return ({ body }: Request, res: Response, next: NextFunction) => { ... } // No logging of orderLinesData

threats.controls_in_place: HTTP access logs via morgan (request-level only)

mitigation.title: Add structured audit logging for B2B orders
steps: Log orderLinesData (sanitized/truncated) along with user ID and IP for each B2B request
Use a structured logging library (e.g., winston) for machine-parseable audit records

threats.title: Dependabot vulnerability pinning suppresses security updates — .dependabot/config.yml
threats.description: Three known-vulnerable packages are explicitly excluded from Dependabot auto-updates: express-jwt@0.1.3 (JWT alg:none bypass), sanitize-html@1.4.2 (XSS bypass), and unzipper@0.9.15 (path traversal). While intentional for training, this creates a permanent CI/CD supply chain risk where newly discovered exploits in these pinned packages can be weaponized without any automated detection.
threats.controls_in_place: Intentional for training — not a defect

mitigation.title: Remove Dependabot ignore rules for vulnerable packages in production deployments
steps: Remove ignore rules from .dependabot/config.yml for production branches
Upgrade express-jwt, sanitize-html, and unzipper to current secure versions
Add npm audit to CI pipeline and fail on high/critical vulnerabilities

threats.title: GitHub Actions not using least-privilege permissions — .github/workflows/ci.yml
threats.description: The CI/CD workflow does not specify a top-level `permissions:` block, defaulting to potentially broad read access to the repository. Some workflow jobs deploy to Docker Hub, Heroku, and GitHub Packages, meaning a compromise of the CI workflow (via unpinned Action substitution) could exfiltrate all repository secrets.

evidence.excerpt: No top-level permissions block; DOCKERHUB_TOKEN, HEROKU_API_KEY, CYPRESS_RECORD_KEY all referenced

threats.controls_in_place: Some actions use pinned SHA (e.g., actions/checkout@11bd71901...)

mitigation.title: Add least-privilege permissions to GitHub Actions workflows
steps: Add `permissions: read-all` at workflow level
Grant specific write permissions only to the jobs that need them (e.g., `packages: write` for Docker publish)
Pin all unpinned third-party actions to SHA digest

threats.title: SQL injection in login — routes/login.ts
threats.description: The login handler constructs a raw SQL query using string interpolation of user-supplied email and password hash values. An attacker can use SQL injection payloads (e.g. `' OR '1'='1'--`) in the email field to bypass authentication entirely and gain access as any user, including admin.

evidence.excerpt: models.sequelize.query(`SELECT * FROM Users WHERE email = '${req.body.email || ''}' AND password = '${security.hash(req.body.password || '')}' AND deletedAt IS NULL`

threats.controls_in_place: None — raw SQL with no parameterization

mitigation.title: Replace raw SQL with parameterized Sequelize query
steps: Replace string interpolation with Sequelize `where` clause using parameterized values
Use `UserModel.findOne({ where: { email, password: hash } })` instead of raw SQL

threats.title: SQL injection in product search — routes/search.ts
threats.description: The search endpoint constructs a raw SQL LIKE query with unescaped user input. An attacker can use UNION-based SQL injection to extract the full database schema and all user data from the SQLite database without authentication.

evidence.excerpt: models.sequelize.query(`SELECT * FROM Products WHERE ((name LIKE '%${criteria}%' OR description LIKE '%${criteria}%') AND deletedAt IS NULL) ORDER BY name`)

mitigation.title: Parameterize the product search query
steps: Replace string interpolation with sequelize.query parameterized replacements
Use `criteria` as a bound parameter: `WHERE name LIKE :search`, `{ replacements: { search: '%' + criteria + '%' } }`

threats.title: Hardcoded RSA private key enables JWT forgery — lib/insecurity.ts
threats.description: The RSA private key used to sign all JWT authentication tokens is hardcoded as a string literal in lib/insecurity.ts. Anyone with access to the source code (public GitHub repository) can use this key to sign JWT tokens as any user, including admin, and authenticate without a valid password.

evidence.excerpt: const privateKey = '-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----\r\nMIICXA...' (truncated — full key in source)

threats.controls_in_place: None — key permanently compromised via public source

mitigation.title: Remove hardcoded RSA private key; use environment variable
steps: Generate new RSA key pair
Store private key in environment variable or secrets manager
Read via `process.env.JWT_PRIVATE_KEY`
Rotate all existing tokens (invalidate current sessions)

threats.title: JWT algorithm none bypass via express-jwt@0.1.3 — lib/insecurity.ts
threats.description: express-jwt version 0.1.3 is vulnerable to JWT algorithm confusion attacks (CVE-2015-9235 class). An attacker can craft a JWT with `alg: none` and an empty signature, which will be accepted by express-jwt@0.1.3 without signature verification. This bypasses authentication entirely.

mitigation.title: Upgrade express-jwt and enforce algorithm whitelist
steps: Upgrade express-jwt to ^8.4.1 (latest)
Explicitly specify algorithms: `expressJwt({ algorithms: ['RS256'] })`
Remove Dependabot ignore rule for express-jwt

threats.title: MD5 password hashing trivially reversible — lib/insecurity.ts
threats.description: All user passwords are hashed with unsalted MD5. MD5 is cryptographically broken and password databases can be cracked entirely using rainbow tables within minutes. Any attacker who obtains a copy of the database has immediate access to all plaintext passwords.

mitigation.title: Replace MD5 with bcrypt or Argon2 for password hashing
steps: Install bcrypt: `npm install bcrypt`
Update UserModel password setter to use bcrypt.hash(password, 12)
Update login query to use bcrypt.compare instead of MD5 comparison
Migrate existing hashes on next user login

threats.title: SSTI + eval() on user-controlled username — routes/userProfile.ts
threats.description: The user profile page uses eval() on a server-side template that includes the user's username. If a username matches the pattern `#{(.*)}`, the captured group is passed to eval() in a Node.js context. An attacker who can set their username to `#{process.mainModule.require('child_process').execSync('cat /etc/passwd')}` achieves server-side code execution.
threats.controls_in_place: None — eval() deliberately retained for challenge

mitigation.title: Remove eval() from template rendering; use safe template substitution
steps: Remove the regex-match + eval() block entirely
Replace with safe string substitution `template.replace(/_username_/g, sanitizedUsername)`
Encode username before template substitution using html-entities

threats.title: Wildcard CORS allows all origins on sensitive endpoints — server.ts
threats.description: The server uses `app.use(cors())` with no origin restriction, allowing any web origin to make cross-origin requests to all API endpoints. Combined with JWT stored in localStorage (not HttpOnly cookie), this enables CSRF-style attacks from malicious websites that can read responses.

mitigation.title: Restrict CORS to known origins
steps: Replace `cors()` with `cors({ origin: ['https://your-domain.com'] })`
For development, use environment variable for allowed origins

threats.title: No rate limiting on login endpoint — server.ts
threats.description: The login endpoint at POST /rest/user/login has no rate limiting. An attacker can perform unlimited brute-force credential attacks. The password hashing uses MD5 (fast) which means brute-force is particularly effective. Combined with the known user emails from /api/Users and hardcoded credentials in users.yml, account compromise is trivial.

evidence.excerpt: app.use('/rest/user/reset-password', rateLimit({...})) // Only reset-password is rate-limited; login is not

threats.controls_in_place: Rate limiting on reset-password and 2FA only

mitigation.title: Add rate limiting to login endpoint
steps: Apply `rateLimit({ windowMs: 15*60*1000, max: 20 })` to POST /rest/user/login
Consider progressive delays after failed attempts
Log failed login attempts for monitoring

threats.title: No CSRF protection on state-changing endpoints — server.ts
threats.description: No CSRF token or SameSite cookie protection is implemented. The cookie-parser middleware uses a weak secret ('kekse'). While JWT in Authorization headers provides some CSRF resistance, endpoints using cookie-based auth (profile page uses req.cookies.token) are vulnerable to CSRF from any origin due to wildcard CORS.
threats.controls_in_place: None — no CSRF tokens, no SameSite cookies

mitigation.title: Implement CSRF protection
steps: Install csurf middleware
Apply CSRF token validation to POST /profile and other state-changing cookie-auth routes
Use SameSite=Strict cookie attribute for token cookie

threats.title: User enumeration via /api/Users — server.ts
threats.description: GET /api/Users returns the full list of all registered user accounts (email, role, profile data) to any authenticated user. This enables enumeration of admin accounts and targeted attacks. The finale-rest auto-generated endpoint excludes password and totpSecret but includes all other fields.
threats.controls_in_place: Requires authentication; password and totpSecret excluded

mitigation.title: Restrict /api/Users to admin role only
steps: Replace `security.isAuthorized()` with `security.isAdmin()` guard
For non-admin users, return only their own user record

threats.title: Unauthenticated product modification via PUT /api/Products/:id — server.ts
threats.description: The authorization middleware for PUT /api/Products/:id is intentionally commented out. Any user (including unauthenticated) can modify product names, descriptions, and prices. This is the changeProduct challenge but represents a real authorization bypass.

mitigation.title: Uncomment authorization middleware for product modification
steps: Uncomment `app.put('/api/Products/:id', security.isAuthorized())`
Consider restricting to admin/accounting role only

threats.title: Confidential files exposed via /ftp directory listing — server.ts
threats.description: The /ftp directory is served with full directory listing enabled. It contains confidential business documents: acquisitions.md (M&A plans), incident-support.kdbx (KeePass password database), coupons_2013.md.bak, and package-lock.json.bak. These files are publicly accessible without authentication.

evidence.excerpt:
# Planned Acquisitions
> This document is confidential! Do not distribute!

threats.controls_in_place: robots.txt disallows /ftp but this is not enforced security

mitigation.title: Remove directory listing; restrict FTP file access
steps: Remove serveIndex for /ftp
Move sensitive files out of web-accessible directories
Implement proper authentication for any files that legitimately need to be downloadable

threats.title: JWT public key and premium.key exposed at /encryptionkeys — server.ts
threats.description: The /encryptionkeys directory is served with directory listing, exposing jwt.pub (the JWT public key, which paired with the hardcoded private key confirms the key pair) and premium.key. While the public key alone is not secret for RS256, this exposure combined with the hardcoded private key in source code means the complete key pair is publicly available.

evidence.excerpt: app.use('/encryptionkeys', serveIndexMiddleware, serveIndex('encryptionkeys', { icons: true, view: 'details' }))

mitigation.title: Remove directory listing for /encryptionkeys
steps: Remove serveIndex for /encryptionkeys
Serve only jwt.pub via a specific endpoint if needed for verification
Move premium.key out of web-accessible directory

threats.title: Access logs exposed via /support/logs directory listing — server.ts
threats.description: The /support/logs directory is served with directory listing, exposing access logs in combined Apache format. These logs reveal all user URLs visited (including order IDs, search queries, user IDs in paths), IP addresses, and user agents. This enables user activity tracking and session correlation attacks.

evidence.excerpt: app.use('/support/logs', serveIndexMiddleware, serveIndex('logs', { icons: true, view: 'details' }))

mitigation.title: Remove public log directory access
steps: Remove serveIndex and serveLogFiles for /support/logs
Restrict log access to admin users only or serve via secure admin panel

threats.title: Prometheus metrics exposed unauthenticated at /metrics — server.ts
threats.description: The Prometheus metrics endpoint is publicly accessible without authentication. It exposes application internals including request counts, durations, error rates, file upload metrics, and startup timing — information that aids attackers in fingerprinting and targeting attacks.

mitigation.title: Restrict metrics endpoint to admin or internal network
steps: Add IP-based restriction or authentication to /metrics endpoint
Alternatively, move metrics to a separate internal port

threats.title: Missing Content-Security-Policy allows unrestricted script execution — server.ts
threats.description: No Content-Security-Policy header is configured. The helmet.xssFilter() middleware is explicitly commented out. The profile page explicitly sets `unsafe-eval` in its per-request CSP to support the SSTI challenge. Without CSP, stored XSS attacks can execute arbitrary JavaScript, load external scripts, and exfiltrate data.

evidence.excerpt: // app.use(helmet.xssFilter()); // = no protection from persisted XSS via RESTful API

threats.controls_in_place: helmet.noSniff() and helmet.frameguard() present; CSP missing

mitigation.title: Configure Content-Security-Policy header
steps: Enable helmet.contentSecurityPolicy() with a restrictive policy
Remove unsafe-eval from profile page CSP
Start with report-only mode to identify violations before enforcing

threats.title: Hardcoded HMAC secret enables coupon forgery — lib/insecurity.ts
threats.description: The HMAC secret used for coupon code generation is hardcoded as 'pa4qacea4VK9t9nGv7yZtwmj'. An attacker who reads the source code can generate valid coupon codes for arbitrary discounts by computing `HMAC-SHA256('pa4qacea4VK9t9nGv7yZtwmj', <data>)`.

mitigation.title: Move HMAC secret to environment variable
steps: Generate new random 256-bit HMAC secret
Store in `process.env.HMAC_SECRET`
Update lib/insecurity.ts to read from environment

threats.title: Open redirect via /redirect?to= parameter — routes/redirect.ts
threats.description: The redirect endpoint validates the target URL against an allowlist, but the validation can be bypassed through subdomain matching or URL manipulation. An attacker can craft phishing links using the trusted Juice Shop domain that redirect to malicious sites.

mitigation.title: Use exact URL matching for redirect allowlist
steps: Replace prefix-based allowlist with exact URL matching
Consider eliminating the open redirect endpoint entirely

threats.title: NoSQL injection via $where clause in product reviews — showProductReviews.ts
threats.description: The showProductReviews handler passes user-supplied product ID directly into a MarsDB `$where` JavaScript evaluation clause. An attacker can inject JavaScript conditions to extract all reviews across all products or trigger sleep-based denial-of-service. The ID is only truncated to 40 chars when the challenge is enabled.
threats.controls_in_place: ID truncation to 40 chars when challenge enabled

mitigation.title: Replace $where with typed equality query
steps: Replace `{ $where: 'this.product == ' + id }` with `{ product: Number(id) }`
Validate id as integer before use

threats.title: NoSQL injection in review update — no author verification — updateProductReviews.ts
threats.description: The updateProductReviews handler accepts `req.body.id` directly as the MarsDB `_id` query without validation. Combined with `multi: true`, an attacker can update ALL reviews in the database simultaneously by passing a wildcard or operator. No author ownership check ensures only the review owner can edit.

evidence.excerpt: db.reviewsCollection.update({ _id: req.body.id }, { $set: { message: req.body.message } }, { multi: true })

mitigation.title: Add author ownership check and remove multi:true
steps: Check that the authenticated user's email matches the review author before allowing update
Remove `multi: true` or require explicit admin flag
Validate and sanitize `req.body.id`

threats.title: User passwords stored as unsalted MD5 — data at rest — models/user.ts
threats.description: The SQLite database stores user passwords as unsalted MD5 hashes. If an attacker gains read access to the database file (via SQL injection, path traversal, or server compromise), all password hashes can be cracked using rainbow tables instantly. Common passwords like 'admin123' would be recovered in milliseconds.

evidence.excerpt: password setter calls security.hash(clearTextPassword) // which is MD5

mitigation.title: Replace MD5 with bcrypt for password storage
steps: Same as M-005 — coordinate with backend auth changes

threats.title: Hardcoded admin credentials in static user data — data/static/users.yml
threats.description: The application seeds the database with hardcoded user credentials from data/static/users.yml. This includes admin@juice-sh.op:admin123, support admin credentials, and multiple customer accounts with passwords. These credentials are in the public GitHub repository and represent permanently compromised accounts.

mitigation.title: Remove hardcoded credentials; use environment-variable-driven seeding
steps: Replace hardcoded admin password with auto-generated password
Store generated admin password in environment variable during setup
Use a first-run setup wizard for non-training deployments

threats.title: SQLite database file on writable filesystem without encryption — data/juiceshop.sqlite
threats.description: The SQLite database file is stored at data/juiceshop.sqlite on the server's filesystem without encryption. Container compromise or path traversal exploits can extract the full database including all user PII, payment card numbers, TOTP secrets, and security answers.

mitigation.title: Enable SQLite encryption or use encrypted database
steps: Consider SQLCipher for encrypted SQLite
Alternatively, store PCI-relevant fields (card numbers) in encrypted columns
Ensure filesystem permissions restrict database file access to the Node.js process user only

threats.title: XXE via XML upload with noent:true — routes/fileUpload.ts
threats.description: The XML upload handler uses libxmljs2 with `noent: true`, which enables external entity processing. An attacker can upload an XML file with an XXE payload pointing to file:///etc/passwd or internal network URLs, causing the server to disclose local file contents or SSRF to internal services.

evidence.excerpt: const xmlDoc = vm.runInContext('libxml.parseXml(data, { noblanks: true, noent: true, nocdata: true })', sandbox, { timeout: 2000 })

mitigation.title: Disable external entity processing in XML parser
steps: Change `noent: true` to `noent: false` in libxmljs2 options
Validate XML against a schema before processing
Consider rejecting XML uploads entirely if the B2B complaint use case can be deprecated

threats.title: Directory traversal in ZIP file upload — routes/fileUpload.ts
threats.description: The ZIP file upload handler extracts entries to uploads/complaints/ without sufficient path validation. The `absolutePath.includes(path.resolve('.'))` check can be bypassed with carefully crafted paths. An attacker can write files to arbitrary locations including ftp/ (e.g. ftp/legal.md), enabling overwrite of sensitive server files.

evidence.excerpt:
const absolutePath = path.resolve('uploads/complaints/' + fileName)
  challengeUtils.solveIf(challenges.fileWriteChallenge, () => { return absolutePath === path.resolve('ftp/legal.md') })

mitigation.title: Use path.resolve with strict prefix validation for ZIP extraction
steps: After resolving the path, verify it starts with `path.resolve('uploads/complaints') + path.sep`
Use the archiver library's safeExtract option or manually validate each entry
Reject ZIP entries with absolute paths or path traversal sequences

threats.title: SSRF via profile image URL upload — routes/profileImageUrlUpload.ts
threats.description: The profile image URL upload endpoint fetches images from user-supplied URLs server-side without validation of the URL scheme or host. An attacker can supply internal URLs (file://, http://169.254.169.254/metadata, http://localhost:3000/metrics) to probe internal services or read local files via the SSRF vector.

evidence.excerpt: Fetches profile image from user-supplied URL without scheme/host validation

mitigation.title: Implement SSRF prevention for URL-based image upload
steps: Validate URL scheme is http:// or https:// only
Implement allowlist of permitted image hosts
Use DNS resolution check to block requests to private IP ranges (RFC 1918)
Consider using a dedicated image proxy service

threats.title: Denial of Service via XML billion-laughs attack — routes/fileUpload.ts
threats.description: The XML parser with `noent: true` is vulnerable to billion-laughs (XML DoS) attacks where entity expansion causes exponential memory allocation. While vm.runInContext has a 2000ms timeout, memory exhaustion can still crash the Node.js process before the timeout triggers.

evidence.excerpt: libxml.parseXml(data, { noblanks: true, noent: true, nocdata: true })

threats.controls_in_place: 2000ms execution timeout (does not prevent memory exhaustion)

mitigation.title: Disable entity expansion and add file size limit
steps: Disable `noent: true` to prevent entity expansion
Enforce maximum XML document size (e.g., 100KB) before parsing
Consider rate-limiting the file upload endpoint

threats.title: Stored XSS via bypassSecurityTrustHtml in admin panel — administration.component.ts
threats.description: The administration component renders user emails and feedback comments using DomSanitizer.bypassSecurityTrustHtml(), which disables Angular's built-in XSS protection. An attacker who registers with an email containing a script tag or injects malicious HTML via feedback can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the browser of any admin who views the administration panel.
threats.controls_in_place: Angular DomSanitizer present but explicitly bypassed

mitigation.title: Remove bypassSecurityTrustHtml calls; use Angular binding safely
steps: Remove bypassSecurityTrustHtml() calls
Use [innerText] binding for email display instead of [innerHTML]
For HTML-containing comments, use a sanitize pipe instead of bypass

threats.title: JWT stored in localStorage — XSS-accessible token storage — login.component.ts
threats.description: The JWT authentication token is stored in localStorage, which is accessible to any JavaScript running on the page. A successful XSS attack can exfiltrate the token via `localStorage.getItem('token')`, allowing session hijacking. HttpOnly cookies would prevent JavaScript access.

mitigation.title: Migrate JWT storage to HttpOnly cookie
steps: Configure backend to set JWT as HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict cookie
Update frontend to remove localStorage usage for token
Remove cookie-parser's plaintext cookie handling for token

threats.title: Client-side only admin guard — Angular route guard bypass — app.guard.ts
threats.description: AdminGuard and AccountingGuard are client-side only Angular route guards. They can be bypassed by directly navigating to /administration or /accounting with a non-admin JWT. The backend has no matching server-side authorization check that restricts the admin UI's data endpoints to admin roles.

mitigation.title: Implement server-side admin role enforcement
steps: Create security.isAdmin() middleware checking JWT role === 'admin'
Apply to all admin-specific REST endpoints (user management, order history, etc.)
Do not rely on client-side guards for security enforcement

threats.title: DOM XSS via search result rendering — search-result.component.ts
threats.description: Search results are rendered using bypassSecurityTrustHtml, allowing stored XSS payloads injected via product names/descriptions (which are updatable by any authenticated user due to PUT /api/Products/:id being unprotected) to execute in the browser of any user who views search results.

mitigation.title: Use safe Angular bindings for search result rendering
steps: Replace bypassSecurityTrustHtml with sanitized output
Use Angular's built-in [innerText] binding for product names/descriptions

threats.title: OAuth token exposed in URL fragment — oauth/oauth.component.ts
threats.description: The OAuth component processes the access token from the URL fragment (window.location.href). URL fragments containing tokens can be leaked via Referer headers, browser history, and server logs. This is the implicit OAuth flow which is deprecated due to token leakage risks.

mitigation.title: Migrate from OAuth implicit flow to authorization code flow with PKCE
steps: Implement PKCE flow instead of implicit flow
Never pass tokens in URL fragments
Use state parameter to prevent CSRF

members.title: Confidential files exposed via /ftp directory listing — server.ts

evidence.excerpt:
# Planned Acquisitions
> This document is confidential! Do not distribute!

members.title: JWT public key and premium.key exposed at /encryptionkeys — server.ts

evidence.excerpt: app.use('/encryptionkeys', serveIndexMiddleware, serveIndex('encryptionkeys', { icons: true, view: 'details' }))

members.title: MD5 password hashing trivially reversible — lib/insecurity.ts
User passwords stored as unsalted MD5 — data at rest — models/user.ts

evidence.excerpt: password setter calls security.hash(clearTextPassword) // which is MD5

members.title: RCE via vm.runInContext sandbox escape in B2B order — routes/b2bOrder.ts
SSTI + eval() on user-controlled username — routes/userProfile.ts
